title: Designing Human and Generative AI Collaboration
study_design_description: An IRB-approved classroom experiment examining effects of different AI collaboration models during creative writing. 285 students participated in a two-day study: Day 1 writing a 1,000-word story without AI assistance (105 minutes), and Day 2 writing a new 1,000-word story with ChatGPT 3.5 assistance (105 minutes) under one of three randomly assigned collaboration models. Day 1 served as a baseline measure of writers' skill level without AI support, while Day 2 analyzed between-subject differences across treatment groups.
primary_task_description: Participants wrote 1,000-word creative fiction stories following a seven-sequence structure commonly used in fiction writing. The creative writing process was divided into four key steps: ideation and outlining (pre-production phase) and drafting and editing (production phase).
population_description: 285 students from a classroom exercise in an elective course on emerging technologies. The cohort showed a higher average Divergent Association Task (DAT) score (82) compared to the general population. Participants' DAT scores ranged between 65 and 95, with a mean of 82.2. Initial survey assessed English proficiency, creative writing skills and experience, and verbal creativity via DAT test.

arms.description: Day 1: participants wrote a 1,000-word story without any AI assistance during a 105-minute session, following a seven-sequence structure commonly used in fiction writing.

AI performs all tasks, and humans either confirm or reject AI-generated output. Participants delegated all tasks, from ideation to editing, to AI. Their role was limited to writing prompts for ChatGPT and deciding whether to accept the output or request a regeneration. During prompt writing, participants were explicitly restricted from introducing their own ideas.

Humans are responsible for pre-production (ideation and outlining) without AI assistance, while the AI executes production phase (drafting and editing) with humans confirming or rejecting AI output. Participants were fully responsible for story ideation and outlining before using ChatGPT to subsequently draft and edit the story. During drafting and editing, participants' roles were limited to writing prompts and deciding whether to accept or regenerate ChatGPT's output.
Humans and AI collaborate throughout the entire process from ideation all the way to editing. Participants could initiate tasks independently or request AI input or feedback at any stage.

outcomes.outcome_description: Time taken for ideation, outlining, drafting, and editing, all of which are self-reported by the participants. Evaluation of whether AI reduced writing time and differences across the treatment groups.
The effectiveness of the story in conveying its main ideas creatively, interestingly, coherently, and with good writing.
The originality of the story, including novel themes, plot, setting, structure, ending, and style.
The story's ability to provide an engaging and immersive experience for readers through literary devices and vivid imagery.
The clarity and comprehensibility of the language, including correct grammar, punctuation, and spelling.
outcomes.measurement_unit: 7-point Likert scale (1=Extremely poor writing quality, 7=Extremely good writing quality)

outcomes.outcome_description: The level of organization and structure in the story.
outcomes.measurement_unit: 7-point Likert scale (1=Extremely poor coherence, 7=Extremely good coherence)

outcomes.outcome_description: Surveys measured satisfaction with the writing process, covering the level of flexibility supported by the process, the effectiveness of the process in helping them achieve their goals, their willingness to use the same writing process again, and overall satisfaction. Additionally, on Day 2, participants rated the effectiveness of and their satisfaction with AI assistance.
How AI changes the type of stories participants write, in terms of story genres and the number of genres per story.
outcomes.measurement_unit: average number of genres per story

outcomes.outcome_description: The similarity between stories generated with AI support, measured as genre similarity.
Aggregate content diversity measured by similarity of stories across participants.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: 36.2% reduction in total completion time, with the most significant gains during the drafting stage (68.6% reduction)
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: participants experienced an average 36.2% reduction in total completion time, with the most significant gains during the drafting stage (68.6% reduction).
In Copilot, while completion times reduced overall, a productivity gap remained across participants (b = 0.70, P < 0.001).
The Human Creativity model eliminated this productivity gap ( b = 0, P  = 0.956), with participants demonstrating similar completion times on Day 2 regardless of their Day 1 completion times.

The Human Confirmation model showed a significant but smaller effect compared to Copilot ( b = 0.32, P  < 0.05), with participants who needed more time on Day 1 continuing to spend more time on the task.

With AI collaboration (Day 2), the Human Confirmation group, where AI managed creative tasks, produced lower-quality stories compared to the Human Creativity (difference = 0.34, P < 0.01) and Copilot groups ( difference = 0.32, P  < 0.01), where humans performed or collaborated in creative tasks.

With AI collaboration (Day 2), the Human Confirmation group, where AI managed creative tasks, produced lower-quality stories compared to the Human Creativity (difference = 0.34, P < 0.01) and Copilot groups ( difference = 0.32, P  < 0.01), where humans performed or collaborated in creative tasks.
No significant differences emerged between the latter two groups.
participants with higher DAT scores initially produced higher quality writing on Day 1, without AI assistance ( b = 0.014, P < 0.05).
However, AI support on Day 2 removed this performance gap based on writer creativity level ( b = -0.001, P = 0.933).

The Human Confirmation group showed the highest average number of genres per story (mean = 4.07, 95% CI = [3.90, 4.23]), followed by Copilot (mean = 3.76, 95% CI = [3.60, 3.92]), Human Creativity (mean = 3.55, 95% CI = [3.40, 3.69]), and writing without AI (mean = 3.45, 95% CI = [3.36, 3.53]).

The Human Confirmation group showed the highest average number of genres per story (mean = 4.07, 95% CI = [3.90, 4.23]), followed by Copilot (mean = 3.76, 95% CI = [3.60, 3.92]), Human Creativity (mean = 3.55, 95% CI = [3.40, 3.69]), and writing without AI (mean = 3.45, 95% CI = [3.36, 3.53]).

The Human Confirmation group showed the highest average number of genres per story (mean = 4.07, 95% CI = [3.90, 4.23]), followed by Copilot (mean = 3.76, 95% CI = [3.60, 3.92]), Human Creativity (mean = 3.55, 95% CI = [3.40, 3.69]), and writing without AI (mean = 3.45, 95% CI = [3.36, 3.53]).
The Human Confirmation group demonstrated significantly higher genre similarity (mean = 0.592, 95% CI = [0.585, 0.598]) compared to writing without AI (mean = 0.532, 95% CI = [0.530, 0.534]).

other conditions which continued to involve human participation in ideation and outlining showed no statistically significant increases in genre similarity compared to writing without AI (Human Creativity: mean = 0.533, 95% CI = [0.526, 0.539]; Copilot: mean = 0.527, 95% CI = [0.521, 0.534]).

other conditions which continued to involve human participation in ideation and outlining showed no statistically significant increases in genre similarity compared to writing without AI (Human Creativity: mean = 0.533, 95% CI = [0.526, 0.539]; Copilot: mean = 0.527, 95% CI = [0.521, 0.534]).

The Human Confirmation group, where AI had the greatest role, showed the highest similarity (mean = 0.779, 95% CI = [0.767, 0.790]), followed by Copilot (mean = 0.743, 95% CI = [0.731, 0.755]), Human Creativity ( mean = 0.710, 95% CI = [0.700, 0.720]), and writing without AI (mean = 0.647, 95% CI = [0.642, 0.653]).

The Human Confirmation group, where AI had the greatest role, showed the highest similarity (mean = 0.779, 95% CI = [0.767, 0.790]), followed by Copilot (mean = 0.743, 95% CI = [0.731, 0.755]), Human Creativity ( mean = 0.710, 95% CI = [0.700, 0.720]), and writing without AI (mean = 0.647, 95% CI = [0.642, 0.653]).

The Human Confirmation group, where AI had the greatest role, showed the highest similarity (mean = 0.779, 95% CI = [0.767, 0.790]), followed by Copilot (mean = 0.743, 95% CI = [0.731, 0.755]), Human Creativity ( mean = 0.710, 95% CI = [0.700, 0.720]), and writing without AI (mean = 0.647, 95% CI = [0.642, 0.653]).

When comparing AI-assisted to human-only writing (Day 2 vs Day 1), the Human Confirmation group showed no improvement in satisfaction, along with decreased flexibility and a reduced willingness to reuse the process.

In contrast, the Human Creativity and Copilot groups experienced increased satisfaction, effectiveness, and intent to reuse the process, with these results being statistically significant (SM Table S.10).

assigning creative tasks to humans while delegating writing and editing tasks to AI (i.e., Human Creativity condition) results in higher scores for interestingness, writing quality, and coherence compared to a fully collaborative approach throughout the entire process (i.e., Copilot condition). However, these differences achieve statistical significance only for coherence (P < 0.05).

notes: Study design: two-day within-subjects and between-subjects design. Day 1 (no AI) serves as baseline; Day 2 participants randomly assigned to one of three AI collaboration models. Total of 285 participants, 549 stories produced, 2,196 evaluations (each story received 4 evaluations). Stories evaluated by 2 internal raters (fellow participants) and 2 external raters (University-recruited) on 1-7 Likert scales. Arm-level sample sizes (n per condition) not explicitly stated in quotes; total N=285 but allocation across three Day 2 conditions not specified. Editing time increased across all three groups on Day 2, possibly because writers were less involved during drafting and needed additional editing to align output with preferences, or because faster earlier stages freed time for editing. Caveats: findings focus on creative writing with ChatGPT 3.5; generalization to other domains (innovation, software development, research) should be cautious. Newer models may yield different results. Study limited to 1,000-word tasks; more complex projects like novels may require different collaboration models. Human Confirmation design showed limitations in creative setting but may be better suited for routine, repetitive tasks. Statistical significance for user satisfaction results referenced in supplementary materials (SM Table S.10) but exact p-values not provided in main quotes.
